title: Pistachio baklava
subtitle: Layered filo, pistachio and walnut, spiced sugar syrup
excerpt: Filo layered with ground pistachios and walnuts, brushed with clarified butter, baked until deeply crisp, then flooded with hot sugar syrup scented with rose water and lemon. The syrup soaks in as it cools; the baklava is best eaten the following day.
sourceNotes: Baklava is documented across the Ottoman culinary tradition from at least the fifteenth century, with earlier references to similar layered sweets in Central Asian and Middle Eastern sources. The version with filo pastry and sugar syrup is described in Ottoman palace cookbooks and spread throughout the regions of the former Ottoman empire. Pistachio baklava from Gaziantep, Turkey, is considered the definitive regional standard. The filo-and-syrup technique is reproduced in countless public-domain sources across Turkish, Greek, and Arab culinary traditions.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Baklava improves after 12–24 hours at room temperature; the syrup distributes evenly through the layers. It keeps in an airtight tin at room temperature for up to 1 week. Do not refrigerate — the filo softens and loses its snap.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven; fan at 150°C. Lower than you might expect — filo burns fast and the layers need time to crisp evenly from base to top.

glossaryTerms.definition: Butter with the milk solids and water removed by gentle heating. It has a higher smoke point than whole butter and applies to filo cleanly without the steam from water content making the pastry soggy. For baklava, heat butter until the foam subsides and spoon off the foam; the clear yellow liquid underneath is clarified butter.

A sugar-and-water syrup cooked to a specific concentration. For baklava, a 1:1 sugar-to-water ratio by weight produces a syrup that soaks into the filo without making it wet or crystallising as it cools. The syrup must be hot when poured over the cold (or room temperature) baklava for correct absorption.

content.text: The two rules of baklava: the filo must be crisp all the way through before the syrup goes on, and the syrup must be hot when poured over the baked pastry. Hot-on-cold or cold-on-hot is the principle — it ensures absorption without sogginess.
brushes on more evenly than whole butter and does not introduce steam that softens the layers. The
goes on immediately after the baklava comes out of the oven.
Clarify the butter and prepare the nuts

Melt the butter in a saucepan over low heat. When the foam rises to the surface, skim it off. The clear yellow liquid beneath is the clarified butter. Allow to cool slightly. Mix the ground pistachios, chopped walnuts, and cinnamon in a bowl.

Preheat the oven to 170°C conventional (150°C fan). Brush a 30 × 20 cm tin generously with clarified butter. Lay filo sheets one at a time into the tin, brushing each with clarified butter. Build up 8 sheets as the base layer. Spread the nut mixture evenly over the base. Layer the remaining filo on top, brushing each sheet with butter. If sheets overhang, tuck them in or trim. Brush the top sheet generously. Using a sharp knife, cut into diamonds or squares all the way through to the base before baking.

Bake for 35–40 minutes until the top is deep amber all over and the pastry is crisp when tapped. While the baklava bakes, make the syrup: combine the sugar and water in a saucepan and bring to a boil, stirring until the sugar dissolves. Simmer for 5 minutes. Add the lemon juice and rose water and keep warm.

Remove the baklava from the oven and immediately pour the hot syrup evenly over the surface, following the cut lines. The sizzling is normal. Leave at room temperature for at least 6 hours before serving; overnight is better. Do not cover — the top must stay crisp.
